title: Cutting a quill pen from a feather
subtitle: How to prepare and cut a goose or turkey feather into a writing instrument
excerpt: Before steel nibs, every pen was cut from a large wing feather. The barrel of the feather is shaped into a nib by a series of cuts with a sharp pen-knife. A well-cut quill outlasts many steel nibs and handles Western broad-edge and pointed-pen work equally well.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on pen-cutting: Johnston describes the quill preparation, hardening, and cutting process in detail. The technique is unchanged from the medieval period and Johnston worked directly from period manuscripts to establish the correct cut sequence. The book is in the public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: A large flight feather (wing or tail) used as a writing instrument. The hollow barrel is cut to form a nib slit and shaped to a broad or pointed edge.
A historical hardening treatment for raw quills involving heat and alum solution. Produces a harder, less springy barrel that holds its shape under writing pressure.
The narrow central cut that divides the tip of the pen into two equal tines, allowing the nib to flex slightly and deliver ink.

content.text: from an untreated feather is too soft to write with for long, the barrel compresses under the pressure of the hand. Edward Johnston, writing in 1906, recommends the

, a soaking and heating process, to harden raw quills. At home scale, cured goose or turkey quills can be bought ready-prepared from specialist craft suppliers, which bypasses the hardening stage and goes straight to cutting.

attrs.title: Cutting tool safety
attrs.body: Quill cutting requires the blade to pass through the barrel toward the non-cutting hand. Hold the quill firmly but with the fingers clear of the cutting path. Work slowly, the cuts are small, and rushing causes the knife to slip.

items.name: Cured goose quill
items.notes: From a specialist calligraphy or natural craft supplier. Second feathers (quills from the second joint of the wing) are most suitable for writing pens; flight feathers from the first joint are stiffer.

items.name: Craft knife with a fresh No. 11 blade
items.notes: A sharp blade is essential. A pen-knife with a stiff back (such as a whittling blade) is the traditional tool.

items.name: Cutting mat, A4
items.notes: For the cutting and paring cuts.

content.text: Remove the barbs and the membrane from the lower part of the quill barrel. Leave the top of the barrel intact, it forms the pen's handhold. Scrape the outside of the barrel with the back of the craft knife to remove any grease. The cleaned barrel should be transparent when held to a light.

Cut the tip of the quill at a diagonal angle of approximately 40-45° from the barrel axis, a long, sloping cut. This establishes the front face of the nib. On the underside (the inside curve of the barrel), make a short scoop cut to remove a small wedge of material, opening a shallow channel. This channel becomes the ink reservoir.
Place the tip of the knife point at the centre of the cut face and press the blade gently down into the quill barrel to split the tip. The

should travel 5-7 mm up the barrel. Too short and the nib won't flex; too long and it flexes too much. The slit should be perfectly central, an off-centre slit produces an unequal thickness on each tine.

Cut across the nib face with a clean horizontal cut to establish the final nib width. Load with a small drop of sumi ink and test on cartridge paper. The pen should produce a thick downstroke and a hairline cross-stroke without catching. If the tines spring apart under pressure, the slit is too long, trim the tip by 1 mm to shorten it.
